title: New York Fed Launches New Research Center for Applied Macroeconomics and Econometrics

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York announced the establishment of the Applied Macroeconomics and Econometrics Center (AMEC) to promote research on macroeconomic models and econometric tools supporting monetary policy evaluation. AMEC will be led by Marco Del Negro and will engage with academics, central banks, and the public on key macroeconomic questions including inequality, financial stability, labor and housing markets, and climate change. The Center will host a symposium on heterogeneity in macroeconomics on November 12.
key_points: AMEC launched November 5, 2021, led by Marco Del Negro as Director with John C. Williams involved
Center's primary research products include: Weekly Economic Index, New York Fed DSGE Model, Treasury Term Premia, Underlying Inflation Gauge, and Yield Curve as Leading Indicator
DSGE model has been used for forecasting since 2011 with real-time public forecasts available
Treasury Term Premia estimates cover maturities 1-10 years from 1961 to present at daily and monthly frequencies
Symposium on 'Heterogeneity in Macroeconomics: Implications for Policy' scheduled for November 12
Research focus areas include inequality, financial stability, labor/housing markets, international economy, climate change, and expectations formation
why_it_matters: This announcement describes the New York Fed's expanded research infrastructure for macroeconomic analysis and forecasting. While it lists research products (DSGE model, term premia estimates, inflation gauges), it does not announce any new policy decisions, rate changes, or market operations. It is an institutional announcement about research capabilities rather than a market-moving macro catalyst.
macro_context: AMEC aims to inform the monetary policy evaluation process through research on macroeconomic models and econometric tools. Research focus includes inequality implications for monetary policy, financial stability, and key gauges used in policy decisions.
